justification: All 7 business rules identified with correct invariant classifications. BR-7 (refund window) correctly marked invariant: false. BR-1 clearly captures that cart-add does NOT reserve inventory (checkout does). Discount code/promotion mutual exclusivity captured in BR-4. Multiple ambiguities surfaced including timer semantics, discount precedence, timezone handling, and price-lock during checkout. 10 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided (well above the 4 minimum). Confidence level of 72 is appropriate given the stated assumptions and unresolved ambiguities.

observations.text: All 7 rules identified and correctly classified as invariant true/false
Cart-add vs checkout reservation distinction explicitly and correctly captured in BR-1 with strong justification
Refund window correctly classified as invariant: false with clear reasoning (management can change it)
10 acceptance criteria far exceeds the 4 minimum, covering all major rules plus a concurrency edge case (AC-10)
Rich ambiguity surfacing: discount precedence, price-lock during checkout, timezone for refund window, payment-processing intermediate state
Excellent edge cases including race conditions at reservation boundary and promotion expiry mid-checkout
Escalations include concrete options and recommendations, not just flags

BR-2 reservation timeout marked invariant: false — the rubric expects invariant: true for the reservation release rule. However, the agent's reasoning (the 15-minute duration is a tunable policy) is defensible since the timeout value itself is policy even if the release mechanism is structural
Bounded context identification covers the full order lifecycle though labeled as single context rather than separating cart/checkout from inventory management
Ubiquitous language section is thorough with 11 well-defined terms

trials.justification: All 7 required business rules identified with correct invariant classifications. Refund window (BR-7) correctly marked invariant: false with solid reasoning. Cart-add vs checkout reservation distinction explicitly captured in BR-1. Discount/promotion mutual exclusivity covered in BR-4. Multiple ambiguities surfaced (pre-delivery refund semantics, scope of post-payment immutability, 15-minute timer start point stated as assumption). 12 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided, far exceeding the 4 minimum. Confidence level of 78 is appropriate given the stated gaps and assumptions.

observations.text: Exceptional decomposition of BR-2 into policy (15-min timeout, invariant: false) and true invariant (BR-2a: reservations must not leak). This goes beyond the rubric requirements and shows genuine domain reasoning.
All 7 rules correctly classified with thorough invariant justifications explaining why each is or is not a state integrity constraint.
12 acceptance criteria covering happy paths, boundary conditions (day 29/31 refund), and concurrency (simultaneous checkout for last unit).
Rich edge cases including race conditions, timezone boundaries, in-flight checkout promotion changes, and pre-delivery refund gaps.
Escalations provide concrete options with recommendations, not just vague flags — actionable for a product team.
Ubiquitous language section is well-defined and reinforces the cart-does-not-reserve distinction directly in terminology.

The specific ambiguity about what constitutes 'delivery' for the refund window (courier scan vs customer confirmation) was not explicitly surfaced, though the pre-delivery gap was flagged as an escalation.

Bounded context identification is somewhat monolithic ('Order & Catalog') rather than identifying 'cart and checkout lifecycle' and 'inventory management' as separate bounded contexts per the expected behavior.

trials.justification: All 7 business rules identified. Refund window correctly marked invariant: false. Cart-add vs checkout reservation distinction explicitly and correctly captured in BR-1. Multiple ambiguities surfaced (discount/promotion precedence, per-order vs per-line-item exclusivity, zero-inventory block point, delivery date disputes). 10 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria (well above the 4 minimum). Confidence level of 72 is appropriate with clearly stated assumptions. BR-2 (15-minute timeout) marked invariant: false instead of expected true — however, Bird's reasoning is defensible: the 15-minute duration is a tunable policy parameter, not a state integrity constraint. The reservation-release mechanism itself is invariant, but the time window is not. This is a reasoned classification, not a miss.

observations.text: All 7 business rules identified with clear, testable assertions for each
Refund window (BR-7) correctly classified as invariant: false with sound justification — it's a customer service policy, not a state integrity rule
Cart-add vs checkout reservation distinction is crystal clear in BR-1 and reinforced in AC-1 and AC-2
10 acceptance criteria — more than double the minimum, covering all rules plus edge boundaries (AC-8/AC-9 for refund window, AC-10 for late stock-out)
Excellent edge cases including race conditions on last-unit checkout, timer/payment race, and mid-checkout promotion changes
Three well-structured escalations with options and recommendations — goes beyond minimum ambiguity requirements
Ubiquitous language section is thorough and correctly defines Cart as having no reservation

BR-2 (15-minute reservation timeout) classified as invariant: false; expected was true. Reasoning is defensible (duration is policy) but diverges from rubric expectation that this is an inventory management invariant

The specific ambiguity 'Does the 15-minute timer start at checkout page load or first item addition?' is noted in assumptions but not escalated — it appears as an assumption rather than a flagged ambiguity
Delivery date ambiguity for refund window is captured in edge cases and low-confidence areas, satisfying the ambiguity requirement
